title: Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Energy intensity represents [primary energy](#dod:primaryenergy) consumption, using the [substitution method](#dod:substitutionmethod), per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita
Energy intensity represents primary energy consumption, using the substitution method, per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between energy intensity and GDP per capita for countries in 2022, plotting energy intensity in kilowatt-hours per dollar on the vertical axis against GDP per capita in international dollars at 2011 prices on a logarithmic horizontal axis. The data points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A broad negative relationship is visible across the chart, where countries with lower GDP per capita generally exhibit higher energy intensity, while nations with higher GDP per capita cluster in the lower energy intensity range. At the upper extreme of energy intensity, Turkmenistan, North Korea, Venezuela, and Bahrain all sit above the 4 kWh per dollar mark, with Turkmenistan reaching the highest visible point on the chart. These high-intensity economies span a wide range of GDP levels, from the lower-income bracket occupied by North Korea to the higher-income bracket of Bahrain. In the middle of the distribution, countries like Russia, Iran, and Ukraine show energy intensity between 2 and 2.5 kWh per dollar. Conversely, the right side of the chart features a dense cluster of high-income nations with low energy intensity. Norway, Qatar, Denmark, and Australia all appear in the band below 1.5 kWh per dollar while maintaining GDP per capita figures exceeding $50,000. African nations such as Burundi, Liberia, and Chad occupy the bottom-left corner, showing both low GDP per capita and relatively low energy intensity compared to the extreme outliers. The visual distribution shows that while economic growth often coincides with reduced energy intensity, specific national circumstances result in significant deviations from this general trend.
